coredata.dc:title: Boric acid modified S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots as simple and inexpensive turn-on fluorescent nanosensor for quantification of glucose
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Masteri-Farahani, Majid
Ghorbani, Fatemeh
Mosleh, Nazanin
coredata.dc:description: A new fluorescent nanosensor based on S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) modified by boric acid was designed for glucose detection. First, the S,N-GQDs was prepared via one pot hydrothermal process utilizing citric acid and thiourea as precursors. Then, S,N-GQDs was modified by boric acid to fabricate (B)/S,N-GQDs. The excitation dependent photoluminescence spectra of (B)/S,N-GQDs confirmed the heteroatom (S,N) dopant effect on GQDs emission. FT-IR and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopies confirmed the modification of S,N-GQDs with boric acid. The optical and electrochemical band gaps of the obtained (B)/S,N-GQDs were found to be 2.7 and 2.5 eV, respectively. The boric acid functionalized S,N-GQDs exhibited fluorescent enhancement at 455 nm upon addition of glucose. Such fluorescence response was used for glucose quantification with a detection limit of 5.5 μM which is comparable with previous boronic acid based fluorescent sensing systems. However, compared with earlier reported expensive boronic acid based glucose sensors, this modified system is simpler, more economical, and efficient. A mechanism was proposed for fluorescence enhancement based on the reaction of cis-diol units of glucose with the boric acid groups of (B)/S,N-GQDs which creates rigid (B)/S,N-GQDs-glucose structures, restricting the non-radiative intramolecular motions and results in the fluorescent enhancement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Boric acid
Modification
Nanosensor
Glucose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of HOMO and LUMO energy states
Glucose sensing in the presence of (B)/S,N-GQDs nanosensor
The functional mechanism of the (B)/S,N-GQDs nanosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support (Grant Number H/4/361 ) from Kharazmi University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top: TEM image (Inset shows the particle size distribution). Bottom: EDX spectrum of the obtained (B)/S,N-GQDs.
FT-IR spectra of thiourea, citric acid, S,N-GQDs, and (B)/S,N-GQDs.
UV–Vis spectra (Inset: magnification of the peaks in the region of 500–700 nm) (left) and Tauc plots (right) of S,N-GQDs and (B)/S,N-GQDs.
Photoluminescence (PL) spectra at different excitation wavelengths (left) and the possible PL emission mechanism (right) of (B)/S,N-GQDs.
CV curves of (B)/S,N-GQDs and bare glassy carbon at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

(a) The fluorescence intensity change of the (B)/S,N-GQDs with different volumes (10–120 μL) of glucose solution (5.5 mM). (b) Linear relationship between the F0/F value and the glucose concentration.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of (B)/S,N-GQDs.
The functional mechanism of the (B)/S,N-GQDs nanosensor.
Comparing the analytical performances of glucose sensing between the present method and some boronic acid based fluorescent nanosensing systems.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: r-QDs, g-QDs: red emitting quantum dot and green emitting quantum dot.

head.ce:title: Boric acid modified S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots as simple and inexpensive turn-on fluorescent nanosensor for quantification of glucose

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Green Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Green Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Chemistry, Kharazmi University, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new fluorescent nanosensor based on S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) modified by boric acid was designed for glucose detection. First, the S,N-GQDs was prepared via one pot hydrothermal process utilizing citric acid and thiourea as precursors. Then, S,N-GQDs was modified by boric acid to fabricate (B)/S,N-GQDs. The excitation dependent photoluminescence spectra of (B)/S,N-GQDs confirmed the heteroatom (S,N) dopant effect on GQDs emission. FT-IR and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopies confirmed the modification of S,N-GQDs with boric acid. The optical and electrochemical band gaps of the obtained (B)/S,N-GQDs were found to be 2.7 and 2.5 eV, respectively. The boric acid functionalized S,N-GQDs exhibited fluorescent enhancement at 455 nm upon addition of glucose. Such fluorescence response was used for glucose quantification with a detection limit of 5.5 μM which is comparable with previous boronic acid based fluorescent sensing systems. However, compared with earlier reported expensive boronic acid based glucose sensors, this modified system is simpler, more economical, and efficient. A mechanism was proposed for fluorescence enhancement based on the reaction of cis-diol units of glucose with the boric acid groups of (B)/S,N-GQDs which creates rigid (B)/S,N-GQDs-glucose structures, restricting the non-radiative intramolecular motions and results in the fluorescent enhancement.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) with highly adjustable fluorescence properties have obtained great interests in various fields. Several metal chalcogenide based QDs have been utilized by various scientific groups for the optical detection of various targets [1–3]. In addition to their utilization as optical sensors, QDs possess certain restrictions such as toxicity, cost, and complicated synthetic methods [4]. Among the various QDs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are novel fluorescent zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials composed of sp2‑carbon domain structures. They show quantum confinement due to their small particle size and possess some advantages such as lower toxicity level, more biocompatibility, and photostability in comparison with heavy metal containing QDs which help to use in the fields of catalysis [5,6], optoelectronics [7], and biomedical imaging [8]. Various GQDs have been prepared and utilized for selective detection of some analytes exploiting photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy [9–13]. However, highly sensitive PL intensity change with low detection limit is still of significant research interest. Several strategies have been reported to improve the PL properties among which doping is one of the effective methods to change and tune the optoelectronic properties of GQDs. Doping with hetero-atoms such as nitrogen [14,15], boron [16], and sulfur [17–21] can effectively modify and improve the optical properties of GQDs to extend their utilization.

Various methods have been introduced for glucose sensing including nanoparticle aggregation-induced colorimetric detection [22–24], optical spectroscopy [25], acoustic [26], and electronic [27] methods. Moreover, significant progress has been attained in the field of electrochemical glucose sensing with simplicity, fast response, and suitability for real-time detection [28–30]. Enzyme-based electrochemical sensors display high sensitivity and selectivity [31,32], however, such natural enzymes suffer from high cost, insufficient long-term stability, and complicated immobilization procedure. Although noble metals or their alloys have been reported to enhance response and selectivity toward non-enzymatic glucose sensing [33–35], the expensive electrode materials prevent their large-scale utilizations. Hence, it is necessary to fabricate non-enzymatic, facile, economical, and fast glucose detection systems. Qu and co-workers employed functionalized GQDs as a fluorescent probe for the selective sensing of glucose where a functionalization process with relatively expensive phenylboronic acid was necessary [36].

Herein, a new nanosensor based on boric acid modified S and N co-doped graphene quantum dots, (B)/S,N-GQDs, is reported for glucose detection. First, S,N-GQDs was chemically prepared in hydrothermal conditions. Incorporation of boric acid groups on the surface of S,N-GQDs facilitates their utilization as a simple and inexpensive PL probe for glucose sensing. It is presumed that the reaction of cis-diol units in glucose with boric acid groups located on the surface of S,N-GQDs generates rigid (B)/S,N-GQDs-glucose structures, restricting the intramolecular motions which results in the enhancement of PL intensity. In contrast to the common aggregation-induced quenching [37,38], an interesting aggregation-induced PL enhancement can be seen for the (B)/S,N-GQDs. The boric acid was selected because of its availability from economical point of view and the one-step surface modification of S,N-GQDs with boric acid groups as one of the most commonly used recognition sites for glucose.
The used materials and equipment were described in detail in supporting information.

Citric acid (0.21 g, 1 mmol) and thiourea (0.23 g, 3 mmol) were dissolved in 5 mL of water and stirred to form a clear solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 160 °C in an electric oven for 4 h. The final product was precipitated by adding 2-propanol into the solution and collected with centrifugation.

First, 1 g of S,N-GQDs was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water. After addition of boric acid (0.124 g), the solution was refluxed for 24 h. The solution was cooled to room temperature and the dark green (B)/S,N-GQDs was precipitated by adding 2-propanol and collected with centrifugation.

To a 2 mL of phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4) of (B)/S,N-GQDs in a quartz cell, various amounts of glucose (5.5 mmol/L, 10–120 μL) were added. The fluorescence spectra were recorded after each addition upon the excitation wavelength of 365 nm.
The S,N-GQDs were synthesized from citric acid and thiourea by simple hydrothermal process and then modified by boric acid according to Scheme 1 .

The morphology and particles size of prepared (B)/S,N-GQDs were investigated with transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis. As seen in Fig. 1 , the TEM image of (B)/S,N-GQDs exhibited the nearly uniform shape and well-distributed size of the (B)/S,N-GQDs. Furthermore, the particle size distribution curve of the obtained (B)/S,N-GQDs was given in Fig. 1 indicating the average size of about 3–4 nm.

The elemental composition of (B)/S,N-GQDs was also investigated by energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis as shown in Fig. 1 where B, C, S, N, and O peaks was observed indicating the successful formation of (B)/S,N-GQDs.

The FT-IR spectra of thiourea, citric acid, S,N-GQDs, and (B)/S,N-GQDs in Fig. 2 show some peaks at about 3000–3500 cm−1 assigned to stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups indicating hydroxyl and amine groups. The bands at 1709 and 1667 cm−1 are respectively attributed to the vibrational stretching band of CO in -COOH and -CONH groups of S,N-GQDs and (B)/S,N-GQDs samples. The peaks at about 1083 cm−1 in FT-IR spectra of thiourea, S,N-GQDs, and (B)/S,N-GQDs can be assigned to the CS stretching vibration. The FT-IR spectrum of the (B)/S,N-GQDs exhibits distinct bands at 1343, 1187, and 1020 cm−1 attributed to BO stretching, B-O-H bending, and B-O-H deformation vibrations, respectively [16,39]. The results indicate that the surface of S,N-GQDs is successfully modified with boric acid groups.

The prepared S,N-GQDs and (B)/S,N-GQDs were dispersed in water to achieve a uniform 0.1 mg/mL solution. Fig. 3 depicts the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the solutions indicating a peak at 330 nm with a shoulder at around 400 nm and a peak at 596 nm. The peaks at 400 and 596 nm can be attributed to n → π* transitions of CO and CS bonds, respectively [18].

The band gap energies (Eg) of S,N-GQDs and (B)/S,N-GQDs can be determined by the following equation:
(1)
αhν
n
= B
hν −
E g
where hν is the photon energy, α is the absorption coefficient, B is a constant relative to the material, and n is 2 for direct transitions as the case of S,N-GQDs. The (αhν)2 versus hʋ curves for the S,N-GQDs and (B)/S,N-GQDs sample were shown in Fig. 3 with band gap energies of about 2.7 eV which is near the reported value [18].

The prepared (B)/S,N-GQDs exhibited an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior (Fig. 4 ), which is possibly attributed to the excitation of different size (B)/S,N-GQDs and different emissive trap sites. As the excitation wavelength increased from 340 to 460 nm, the emission peak position shifted to longer wavelength and the PL intensity decreased. This excitation dependent PL property may be explained by the particle size variation and different orbital energies of the defects states. Size variation in (B)/S,N-GQDs generates separate sp2 related localized states. The electronic transitions from such localized states are responsible for shifts in PL emission. Furthermore, the various functional groups at the edge of (B)/S,N-GQDs may produce trap states between HOMO and LUMO levels which results in Stokes shift in emission upon excitation [40,41]. On the basis of these results and earlier reports on N-doped GQDs [42,43], a model for the PL mechanism can be proposed to explain the PL process of (B)/S,N-GQDs (Fig. 4). If the excitation wavelength is below 400 nm, the CO containing chromophores are excited and blue light is emitted from the (B)/S,N-GQDs. If the excitation wavelength is about 420 nm, the light is absorbed by the CN containing chromophores and green light emitted. We also observed a mixture of blue and green light emitted from (B)/S,N-GQDs under excitation wavelength of 440–460 nm. Moreover, sulfur doping induces the insertion of the CS π* orbital below the CN π* orbital due to the lower electronegativity of sulfur [44,45]. Thus, it can absorb light with a lower energy and emit light with a longer wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of HOMO and LUMO energy states
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry was utilized to directly determine the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of the (B)/S,N-GQDs and the corresponding band gap energy. Using potentials above the LUMO or below the HOMO results in the emergence of cathodic and anodic currents, respectively. The electrode for electrochemical analysis was prepared by drop-casting the (B)/S,N-GQDs suspension (5 μL) and Nafion (0.5 μL, 5% w) onto a glassy carbon electrode. The oxidation and reduction peaks were consequently utilized to determine the EHOMO and ELUMO values using the following three equations [46]:

The (B)/S,N-GQDs/glassy carbon electrode shows a reduction peak at −0.5 V and an oxidation peak at +1.9 V versus Ag/AgCl. The ELUMO and EHOMO values calculated from these peaks are ELUMO = −4.053 eV and EHOMO = −6.553 eV versus vacuum, respectively. The band gap energy determined from the cyclic voltammetry was 2.5 eV, which is in a good agreement with that obtained from the optical absorption spectroscopy (2.7 eV) (Fig. 5 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose sensing in the presence of (B)/S,N-GQDs nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 shows the change in the PL spectra of the (B)/S,N-GQDs upon addition of glucose. The fluorescence intensity of the (B)/S,N-GQDs at 455 nm is highly sensitive to glucose and increases as the concentration of glucose is increased. The corresponding Stern-Volmer plot shows a good linear relationship (R = 0.987) between the fluorescence intensities and the glucose concentrations (5.5 mM) in the volume range from 10 to 120 μL with a detection limit of 5.5 μM.

The performance of prepared nanosensor was compared with other analytical systems for determination of glucose and the results were given in Table 1 . This method is rapid, simple, and low cost when compared to some earlier methods. Although determination of glucose with this procedure is indirect, but it has a good detection limit and wide linear range in comparison with some other direct methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The functional mechanism of the (B)/S,N-GQDs nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of above experimental results and previous reports [16,50], a functional mechanism for the PL response of (B)/S,N-GQDs to glucose can be proposed as shown in Scheme 2 . Interaction of the cis-diol units of glucose with boric acid groups of the (B)/S,N-GQDs generates (B)/S,N-GQDs-glucose aggregates in which (B)/S,N-GQDs are connected by a glucose linker. The high energy barrier for intramolecular motions blocks the non-radiative relaxation mechanism and favors the radiative decay which results in enhancing the PL emission [50–52]. The possibility of aggregate formation increases with increasing glucose concentration. The presence of cis-diol units in glucose is the key structural feature for the glucose-specific response of (B)/S,N-GQDs.

In summary, a novel nanosensor based on the modification of S,N-GQDs with boric acid groups was designed which can provide significant utilization in simple, inexpensive, and selective glucose sensing. It works on the basis of a conceptually new mechanism i.e. the PL intensity of (B)/S,N-GQDs is enhanced by the restriction of the intramolecular motions by the (B)/S,N-GQDs-glucose interactions. The common aggregation-induced quenching process is not preferred in practice because there are a variety of solutes or solvents that may interfere with quenching and leading to false positive results. However, the reaction of two cis-diol units in glucose with two boric acid groups on the (B)/S,N-GQDs surface produces structurally rigid (B)/S,N-GQDs-glucose aggregates which results in an enhancement of PL intensity.
Majid Masteri-Farahani: Supervisor, editing the manuscript.
Fatemeh Ghorbani: Investigation, data collection.
Nazanin Mosleh: Writing, draft preparation, investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support (Grant Number H/4/361) from Kharazmi University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118892.
